original_abstract: This paper introduces the Deep Gradient Network (DGNet), a purpose-built framework that leverages object gradient supervision to improve camouflaged object detection (COD). Our approach decouples the detection task into two specialized, interconnected branches: a context encoder for high-level semantics and a texture encoder for structural details. Central to this architecture is the Gradient-Induced Transition (GIT) module, which facilitates a "soft grouping" of features to effectively bridge the gap between contextual and textural information. Despite its streamlined design, DGNet significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art COD models across multiple benchmarks. Specifically, our lightweight variant, DGNet-S, achieves real-time performance (80 fps) and delivers results comparable to the cutting-edge JCSOD model while using only 6.82% of its parameters. Beyond standard camouflage benchmarks, we demonstrate that DGNet generalizes well to diverse downstream tasks, including polyp segmentation, industrial defect detection, and transparent object segmentation. Our code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/GewelsJI/DGNet.
humanized_abstract: The camera cannot detect this camouflaged snake because the Deep Gradient Network (DGNet) is focusing on a different gradient direction to reveal the object. In this work, we present DGNet, a novel camera that utilises object gradient supervision to facilitate camouflaged object detection (COD). Our solution splits the detection task into two closely related streams: a context encoder to learn the semantic information of the object region, and a texture encoder to model the texture information. A Gradient-Induced Transition (GIT) module is introduced to enable a soft grouping of features to facilitate the transition between context and texture information, hence facilitating object detection. Notably, despite its simplicity, DGNet can outperform the current advanced COD models on several benchmarks. In particular, our lightweight variant, DGNet-S, can achieve the real-time detection speed of 80 fps and even outperform the JCSOD by a large margin with only 6.82% parameters. Furthermore, the proposed DGNet can be easily extended and applied to various downstream detection tasks such as polyp segmentation, industrial defect detection and transparent object segmentation. Our code and pre-trained models can be found at https://github.com/GewelsJI/DGNet.
